revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = [[File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja 20190814b.jpg|250px|Satomiya Shrine Hall]]
Haiden (Satomiya) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=|marker=}} |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}
{{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}
Miyoshi Reijin |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}} '''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社[[ja:太平山三吉神社]]) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice. == History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory, dedicating a kabura arrow. Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka. The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread. == festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine * May 8: Spring Grand festival * July 17: Opening Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival * September 17: Closing Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival == Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) * Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin * founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami * founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami * founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya == Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. == Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain. By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City. The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|title= 東北霊山と修験道 |year= 1979 |publisher= 名著出版 |series= 山岳宗教史研究叢書 7 |isbn= 978-4626001702 |ref= {{SfnRef|東北霊山と修験道}} }} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|title= 秋田大百科事典 |year= 1981 |publisher= [[秋田魁新報社]] |isbn= 4870200074 }} == Related Articles == {{commonscat|Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja}} * [[Mount Taihei (Akita)|Mount Taihei]] * {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} * [[Akita City]] * {{ill|Miyoshi-oni|ja|三吉鬼}} [[Category:Unsorted Completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted Completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted Completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine]]== External Links == Akita Prefecture Shrine Office * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200026_00000　Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Bonten festival] NHK (video) {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = [[File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja 20190814b.jpg|250px|Satomiya Shrine Hall]]
Haiden (Satomiya) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=|marker=}} |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}
{{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}
Miyoshi Reijin |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}} '''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社[[ja:太平山三吉神社]]) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice. == History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory, dedicating a kabura arrow. Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka. The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread. == festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine * May 8: Spring Grand festival * July 17: Opening Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival * September 17: Closing Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival == Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) * Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin * founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami * founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami * founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya == Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. == Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain. By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City. The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = [[File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja 20190814b.jpg|250px|Satomiya Shrine Hall]]
Haiden (Satomiya) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=|marker=}} |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}
{{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}
Miyoshi Reijin |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}} '''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社[[ja:太平山三吉神社]]) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice. == History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory, dedicating a kabura arrow. Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka. The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread. == festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine * May 8: Spring Grand festival * July 17: Opening Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival * September 17: Closing Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival == Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) * Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin * founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami * founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami * founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya == Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. == Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain. By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City. The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|title= 東北霊山と修験道 |year= 1979 |publisher= 名著出版 |series= 山岳宗教史研究叢書 7 |isbn= 978-4626001702 |ref= {{SfnRef|東北霊山と修験道}} }} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|title= 秋田大百科事典 |year= 1981 |publisher= [[秋田魁新報社]] |isbn= 4870200074 }} == Related Articles == {{commonscat|Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja}} * [[Mount Taihei (Akita)|Mount Taihei]] * {{ill|Bonten Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine] - Akita Prefecture Shrine Office * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200026_00000　Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Bonten festival] NHK (video) {{Shinto Horizontal}} Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread. == festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine * May 8: Spring Grand festival * July 17: Opening Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival * September 17: Closing Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival == Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) * Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin * founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami * founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami * founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya == Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. == Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain. By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City. The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = [[File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja 20190814b.jpg|250px|Satomiya Shrine Hall]]
Haiden (Satomiya) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=|marker=}} |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}
{{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}
Miyoshi Reijin |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}} '''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社[[ja:太平山三吉神社]]) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice. == History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory, dedicating a kabura arrow. Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka. The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread. == festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine * May 8: Spring Grand festival * July 17: Opening Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival * September 17: Closing Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival == Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) * Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin * founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami * founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami * founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya == Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. Haiden (Satomiya) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=|marker=}} |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}
{{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}
Miyoshi Reijin |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}} '''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社[[ja:太平山三吉神社]]) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice. == History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory, dedicating a kabura arrow. Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka. The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread. == festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine * May 8: Spring Grand festival * July 17: Opening Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival * September 17: Closing Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival == Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) * Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin * founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami * founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami * founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya == Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. == Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain. By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City. The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|title= 東北霊山と修験道 |year= 1979 |publisher= 名著出版 |series= 山岳宗教史研究叢書 7 |isbn= 978-4626001702 |ref= {{SfnRef|東北霊山と修験道}} }} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|title= 秋田大百科事典 |year= 1981 |publisher= [[秋田魁新報社]] |isbn= 4870200074 }} == Related Articles == {{commonscat|Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja}} * [[Mount Taihei (Akita)|Mount Taihei]] * {{ill|Bonten Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine] - Akita Prefecture Shrine Office * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200026_00000　Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Bonten festival] NHK (video) {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = [[File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja 20190814b.jpg|250px|Satomiya Shrine Hall]]
Haiden (Satomiya) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=|marker=}} |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}
{{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}
Miyoshi Reijin |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}} '''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社[[ja:太平山三吉神社]]) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice. == History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory, dedicating a kabura arrow. Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka. The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread. == festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine * May 8: Spring Grand festival * July 17: Opening Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival * September 17: Closing Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival == Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) * Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin * founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami * founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami * founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya == Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. == Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain. By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City. The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = [[File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja 20190814b.jpg|250px|Satomiya Shrine Hall]]
Haiden (Satomiya) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=|marker=}} |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}
{{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}
Miyoshi Reijin |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}} '''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社[[ja:太平山三吉神社]]) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice. == History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory, dedicating a kabura arrow. Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka. The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread. == festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine * May 8: Spring Grand festival * July 17: Opening Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival * September 17: Closing Mountain festival (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival == Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) * Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin * founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami * founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) * Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami * founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) * Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami * founded: Unknown File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya == Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine. == Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}. The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain. By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City. The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}. In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房. In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}. During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}. == Cultural Properties == {{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine ranking system == Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine ** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]] == Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}} == {{神社 |名称 = 太平山三吉神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja 20190814b.jpg|250px|里宮社殿]]
拝殿（里宮）
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=|marker=}} |所在地 = [[秋田県]][[秋田市]][[広面]]字赤沼3-2 | 緯度度 = 39|緯度分 = 43|緯度秒 = 34.2 | 経度度 = 140|経度分 = 08|経度秒 = 39.8 |祭神 = [[大国主|大己貴大神]]
[[スクナビコナ|少彦名大神]]
三吉霊神 |社格 = 旧[[県社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建 = 伝[[白鳳]]2年（673年） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[5月8日]]、[[10月17日]] |神事 = |地図=Japan Akita }} {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=太平山三吉神社|width=256}} {{座標一覧}} '''太平山三吉神社'''（たいへいざんみよしじんじゃ）は、[[秋田県]][[秋田市]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。山岳信仰の対象。[[太平山 (秋田県)|太平山]]のふもとに里宮が、山頂に奥宮がある。 == 祭神 == * [[大国主|大己貴大神]]（おおなむちのかみ） * [[スクナビコナ|少彦名大神]]（すくなびこなのおおかみ） * '''三吉霊神'''（みよしのおおかみ） 三吉霊神は太平の城主、藤原三吉（鶴寿丸）が神格化されたと社伝にあるが、基本的に[[山岳信仰]]による神である。力、勝負、破邪顕正を司る神である[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御祭神] - 太平山三吉神社。 == 由緒 == 社伝によれば[[白鳳]]2年（[[673年]]白鳳は[[逸年号]]。一般的にいわれる白鳳と[[西暦]]の対照表と合っていないが、社伝ではこうなっている。）、[[役小角]]が創建したという田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』。その後[[延暦]]20年（[[801年]]）[[征夷大将軍]][[坂上田村麻呂]]が戦勝を祈願して社殿を建立し、鏑矢を奉納したという。 中世を通じて[[薬師如来]]を[[本地仏]]とする[[修験道]]の霊場として崇敬され、近世には[[秋田藩]]主[[佐竹氏]]からも社領を寄進され、現在の里宮は8代曙山[[佐竹義敦]]の建立である。[[戊辰戦争]]では[[奥羽鎮撫総督]] [[九条道孝]]の祈願所となった。 現在も東北地方を中心として全国に三吉神社が祀られ、太平山講、三吉講も広く分布している。 == 祭事 == * [[1月17日]]：[[梵天まつり (秋田県)|三吉梵天祭]][http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - 太平山三吉神社 * [[5月8日]]：春の例大祭 * [[7月17日]]：[[開山祭]]（御神体が里宮から奥宮（太平山山頂）へ遷る） * [[8月8日]]：講社大祭 * [[9月17日]]：閉山祭（御神体が奥宮から里宮へ遷る） * [[10月17日]]：秋の例大祭 == 境内外社 == * '''奥宮'''：（秋田市[[太平山谷]]字谷山21、秋田市[[仁別]]字務沢、[[太平山 (秋田県)|太平山]]山頂{{ウィキ座標|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【地図】|name=太平山三吉神社奥宮}}） :祭神：大己貴大神、少彦名大神、三吉霊神 :創建：伝白鳳2年 * '''木曽吉山神社'''：（秋田市[[太平八田]]字矢櫃、中岳山頂{{ウィキ座標|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【地図】|name=木曽吉山神社}}） :祭神：[[金山彦神|金山比古神]]、[[金山姫神|金山比賣神]] :創建：[[弘化]]3年（[[1846年]]） * '''神仙山神社'''：（秋田市太平八田字矢櫃、前岳山頂{{ウィキ座標|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【地図】|name=神仙山神社}}） :祭神：[[事代主神]]、[[オオワタツミ|大綿積神]]、[[猿田彦神]] :創建：[[弘化]]年間以前 * '''御手洗神社'''：（秋田市仁別字務沢1） :祭神：[[カグツチ|火産霊神]]、[[祓戸神]] :創建：不明 * '''御滝神社'''：（秋田市仁別字務沢） :祭神：[[カグツチ|火産霊神]]、祓戸神 :創建：不明 ファイル:太平山神社.jpg|太平山三吉神社 奥宮 ==参籠所== 太平山頂の奥宮に隣接して参籠所（山小屋）があり、夏季は神社関係者が常駐する。宿泊・食事は要予約。休憩無料[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - 太平山三吉神社。 == 太平山信仰 == [[太平山 (秋田県)|太平山]]は山そのものが神である。その三吉霊神は力の神、勝利の神、事業の神、農耕の神、受験の神である。修行のために太平山にこもり修業によって自ら神になったとされる。[[菅江真澄]]は「勝手能雄弓（かってのおゆみ）」で、[[鎌倉時代]]に[[鳥海山]]麓の矢島から大江氏が居住していた。その一族が太平に移住して永井氏と名乗ったが、一帯を旧氏名にあやかって大江平（おおえだいら）と言った。それがなまった大平（おえだら）となったという説を紹介している。現在でも地元民は「おえだら」という。この説を延長するとおえだらの山すなわち太平山となる。太平山は大江氏を通して薬師如来とされた。これは鳥海山から来たもので、鳥海山も薬師如来である。佐藤久治は太平山と三吉野（[[吉野山]]）との類似性を指摘して、三吉はもともと三吉野から来たと推定している{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}。 太平山の山名が固定された時代は定かではない。金峰山、薬師峰、三本ヵ岳、大蛇峯（おろちね）などともよばれた。太平山信仰が一般化したのは江戸中期である。村々に講中ができ板碑が建てられたのは文化文政年間（1803年-30年）である。登山者が多い割には特にガイドを必要とせず、したがって登山口に修験集団はできなかった。修験は登山口で神社の別当をするにとどまった。太平山や三吉さんが全国的に広がったのは銘酒太平山の宣伝広告に平行し、正月の祭典である梵天が珍祭として観光資源となったことが大きく影響を及ぼしている{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}。 山頂の太平山三吉神社奥宮は祀神が[[大己貴命]]、[[スクナビコナ]]、三吉霊神である。白鳳2年に[[役小角]]が創立し、延暦2年に[[坂上田村麻呂]]が再興したと伝わる。明治6年に郷社、明治12年に県社、明治39年に'''太平山三吉神社'''となり、大正2年に赤沼に里宮の設置許可が降りる。6月28日に太平山参がある。江戸時代末期には爆発的な太平山信仰の広がりがあった。各地の修験を仲介に講中ができ、三吉神社あるいは太平山神社を作った。しかし、多くは小堂や石堂である。89ヶ所もの社が佐竹藩六郡に広がっていた。由利郡と本庄市には4社ほどあった。また、男鹿市にはまったくなく、南秋田郡には2社しかなかった。これは男鹿の[[真山神社|真山]]本山信仰圏には浸透できなかったものと考えられる。[[1910年]]（明治43年）前後の神社合併促進運動のため多くの社は合併されていった。また、標高500m以下の山を「太平山」として土地の修験者は修験の場として講中はその山に登って太平山信仰の代わりにした。これがミニ太平山である。[[河辺郡]][[雄和町]]や[[仙北郡]][[西仙北町]]、[[大曲市]]の姫神山、[[雄勝郡]][[羽後町]]、[[山本郡]][[二ツ井町]]に5山があり（いずれも旧町名）すべて太平山の名称で呼ばれている{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=279-280}}。 三吉神の最も古い記録は、[[只野真葛]]や[[菅江真澄]]のものがある。江戸女流文学者の[[只野真葛]]が著した『むかしばなし』では三吉鬼は「見知らぬ男」と言われている。酒屋で酒を飲んで、そのまま出ていこうとするが、そこで男に酒代を請求すると必ず災いに遭い、酒を捧げると酒代十杯ほどの薪が門に積み上がっている。それからは、その男だろうと酒をあくまで飲ませれば必ず夜中に代わりの物が積み上がっているので、誰が言うと無く「三吉鬼」と呼んだ。後にはどこかの山の大松をこの庭に移してくれと願をかけて酒樽をささげると酒は無くなり一夜のうちに松の木が庭に植えられている。大名も人力で動かせない品を酒を出して願うと、願いに従って運ぶ。三吉鬼三吉鬼ともてはやしたが、文化年中より三～四十年前より絶えてその者は人里に出なくなってしまった只野真葛『むかしばなし』、平凡社、[[1984年]]、p.200-201『[{{NDLDC|970597/211}} 昔はなし]』仙台叢書. 第9巻収録、仙台叢書刊行会、大正11-15年、p.396。[[菅江真澄]]は『月酒遠呂智泥』で、ある年仙北郡のなる外大伴村（[[外小友村]]）で相撲取りをして世を渡っている若者が、太平山に登り三吉神に酒や[[餅|粢]]を供えることでカ士は力を得ている「月のおろちね」、菅江真澄遊覧記 5 収録、[[1968年]]、p.174-175。三吉神の力の神としての性格がうかがえる。一方「三吉」の所在を尋ねられた籠舎にいた人々が「神仙であるからどことも定まっていない」と答えていることにも注目すると、太平山村近の人々は三吉神が神仙であると認識していることになる。太平山三吉神社に保存されている棟札にも「仙人三吉権現」（[[1691年]]）と記されていることから、三吉神は元々仙人と考えられていたと推測できる{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}。 太平山の神を三吉（みつよし）大権現として、近頃西蝦夷地（北海道）の場所場所で大いに信仰している。元は増慶（毛？）から発祥したと由縁を聞いたが長いので省略する。[[増毛郡|増毛]]の運上屋で甲子の年に神社を新しく建て勧請した。利尻島の運上屋も乙丑の年に神社を建てて祀ったという。浜に居住する漁師も、増毛あたりは10に6から7は信仰している。秋田の[[太平山]]は薬師を祀っている。あるいは三吉という神仙を伝えている。三吉と唱えることは楢山村の百姓伝之助というものが、近年木曽石に新たに神社を建てて祀った時に、彼が初めて唱えたことである。廃藩置県となって、新規の神社になると伝之助が鎮守となった石井忠行『伊頭園茶話 二の巻』、「新秋田叢書 第7巻」収録、p.109。 近世期なかばに、太平山信仰は[[秋田藩]]（久保田）内の庶民の間に定着した。そして、文化文政年間には各村で太平山信仰の講が組織されるようになった。その伝統は現在も命脈を保っており、平成11-12年([[1999年]]-[[2000年]])の時点で、太平山三吉神社総本宮（奥宮・里宮）に定期的に参拝を行う講が85団体、同じく三吉神社総本古が16団体存在した（合計101団である。これらの約8割が秋田県内で組織された講であることから、太平山は、主に秋田県の人々が信仰を寄せる霊山だと言えよう。一つの太平山信仰の講社には約15世帯が加入しており、一般に、戸主（男性）がその活動に参加することになっている『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[2014年]]、p.106－109、柏書房。 太平講の秋田市外旭川の梶目地区の[[1979年]]の実態は「夏季の太平山登山を目的にした団体であったが、次第に信仰は薄れ楽しみ会に移行している。8人ほどの会員が、1ヶ月に1回夜間に十月は昼から盛大に持ち回りで飲食を行う。床間には三吉大明神の掛物をかけ『ロッコンザイショウ　太平山三吉ノ権現　ザンギザンギ』と唱える。もともとは夕方法螺貝を吹き集会をして、登山の時は精進をして魚肉を絶ち水浴び（垢離取り）をした。正月の梵天あげと、近隣への梵天参加が最大行事である」というものであった{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|p=281}}。八郎潟周辺の事例では、八郎潟周辺に太平講は広く存在していた。[[琴浜村]]野石八幡神社の境内には[[1858年]]と記録された太平山碑がある。太平山神像は薬師像ではなく斧を持った山神像そっくりである。[[鹿渡町]]の『太平山議員名簿』の規約によれば「毎月13日に集まって遥拝する。旧1月17日に太平山三吉神社に、旧7月14日に太平山まで1名以上が参拝すること」となっている。17日は太平山三吉神社の縁日であって、その日に会合を持つ例が多く、17日講とも言われている。[[八竜村]]追泊では11名の会員が17日に会合を行い「さんぎ　さんぎ　六根さんぎ　お山八大金剛童子」と唱える。戦中・戦後の頃から三吉神社への参拝だけの活動になっていたが、最近はそれすらも少なくなっている『八郎潟の研究　第１輯』、秋田県水産試験場、[[1954年]] 、p.913。[[上小阿仁村]]での太平山講は地区によって違っており、料理をもちより地区の太平山の祠にお参りをしたり、太平山まで行き参拝して帰りは萩形で一泊する地区などがあった東洋大学民俗研究会『上小阿仁の民俗』、[[1980年]]、p.319。 [[秋田戦争]]時には修験僧が太平山に籠もって朝敵退散の修法をしている。飯田川の[[小玉醸造]]は酒造業をはじめ「金剛」という銘酒を売り出した。最初は好評だったが2・3年にして販路が止まった。ある夜「太平山に登れ」という夢告があり、はたと思いついて銘酒を「太平山」と改名したところ、需要は急増し全国的な銘酒に成長した。そして太平山の名がこの銘酒によって広がる結果ともなった{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|p=282}}。 == 文化財 == {{節スタブ}} == 年表 == 〈〉は関連事項 * [[白鳳]]2年（[[673年]]）：伝役小角創建 * [[延暦]]20年（[[801年]]）：伝坂上田村麻呂社殿建立 * [[慶長]]7年（[[1602年]]）：〈[[佐竹義宣 (右京大夫)|佐竹義宣]]、秋田入封。〉 * [[明治]]元年（[[1868年]]）3月：〈[[神仏分離令]]〉 * 明治4年（[[1871年]]）5月14日：〈[[近代社格制度|社格制度]]制定〉 == 交通 == [[秋田中央インターチェンジ|秋田中央IC]]から約4キロメートル、[[秋田県道28号秋田岩見船岡線]]沿い === バス === * 三吉神社入口 停留所（[[秋田駅]]から大学病院線・太平八田線）[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - 太平山三吉神社 ** [[秋田中央交通]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|title= 東北霊山と修験道 |year= 1979 |publisher= 名著出版 |series= 山岳宗教史研究叢書 7 太平山三吉神社] - 秋田県神社庁 * [https://www2.nhk.or.jp/archives/movies/?id=D0004200026_00000　太平山三吉神社梵天祭] [[日本放送協会|NHK]] (video) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:たいへいさんみよししんしや}} [[Category:秋田県の神社]]
