<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11443949]] Wikidata name: Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja Shaden.jpg |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita |location = {{ill|Akita|WD=Q17139}} |coordinates = {{Coord|39.726167|N|140.144389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}}
'''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)<ref name="akita-jinja">田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』</ref><ref name=yuisho>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice<ref name=yuisho/>.
== History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu<ref name=akita-jinja/><ref name=yuisho/><ref name="akita-shinkou">斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』</ref>. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory<ref name=akita-jinja/>, dedicating a kabura arrow<ref name=yuisho/>.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity<ref name=yuisho/>. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu<ref name=akita-jinja/>. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka<ref name=yuisho/>.
The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide<ref name=akita-jinja/>. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread<ref name=akita-shinkou/><ref name=yuisho/>.
== festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bonten Matsuri|1=Bonten Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538775}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)<ref name=matsuri>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> * May 8: Spring Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * July 17: Opening Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * September 17: Closing Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/>
== Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) ** Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin ** founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami ** founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami ** founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown
<gallery> File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya </gallery>
== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge<ref>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>.
== Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain.
By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City.
The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691<ref>{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}</ref>.
In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、[[:en:2014|2014]]、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties ==
{{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu<ref name=yuisho/> * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro<ref name=yuisho/> * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine Ranking system
== Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)<ref name=access>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> *** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]]

During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、{{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014}}、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11443949]] Wikidata name: Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja Shaden.jpg |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita |location = {{ill|Akita|WD=Q17139}} |coordinates = {{Coord|39.726167|N|140.144389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}}
'''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)<ref name="akita-jinja">田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』</ref><ref name=yuisho>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice<ref name=yuisho/>.
== History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu<ref name=akita-jinja/><ref name=yuisho/><ref name="akita-shinkou">斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』</ref>. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory<ref name=akita-jinja/>, dedicating a kabura arrow<ref name=yuisho/>.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity<ref name=yuisho/>. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu<ref name=akita-jinja/>. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka<ref name=yuisho/>.
The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide<ref name=akita-jinja/>. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread<ref name=akita-shinkou/><ref name=yuisho/>.
== festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bonten Matsuri|1=Bonten Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538775}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)<ref name=matsuri>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> * May 8: Spring Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * July 17: Opening Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * September 17: Closing Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/>
== Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) ** Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin ** founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami ** founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami ** founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown
<gallery> File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya </gallery>
== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge<ref>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>.
== Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain.
By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City.
The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691<ref>{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}</ref>.
In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、{{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014}}、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties ==
{{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu<ref name=yuisho/> * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro<ref name=yuisho/> * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine Ranking system
== Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)<ref name=access>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> *** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]]

The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain.
By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City.
The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691<ref>{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}</ref>.
In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、{{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014}}、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties ==
{{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu<ref name=yuisho/> * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro<ref name=yuisho/> * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine Ranking system
== Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)<ref name=access>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> *** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja Shaden.jpg |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita |location = {{ill|Akita|WD=Q17139}} |coordinates = {{Coord|39.726167|N|140.144389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}}
'''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)<ref name="akita-jinja">田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』</ref><ref name=yuisho>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice<ref name=yuisho/>.
== History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu<ref name=akita-jinja/><ref name=yuisho/><ref name="akita-shinkou">斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』</ref>. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory<ref name=akita-jinja/>, dedicating a kabura arrow<ref name=yuisho/>.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity<ref name=yuisho/>. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu<ref name=akita-jinja/>. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka<ref name=yuisho/>.
The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide<ref name=akita-jinja/>. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread<ref name=akita-shinkou/><ref name=yuisho/>.
== festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bonten Matsuri|1=Bonten Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538775}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)<ref name=matsuri>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> * May 8: Spring Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * July 17: Opening Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * September 17: Closing Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/>
== Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) ** Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin ** founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami ** founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami ** founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown
<gallery> File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya </gallery>
== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge<ref>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>.
== Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain.
By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City.
The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691<ref>{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}</ref>.
In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、{{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014}}、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties ==
{{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu<ref name=yuisho/> * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro<ref name=yuisho/> * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine Ranking system
== Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)<ref name=access>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> *** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]]

Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice<ref name=yuisho/>.
== History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu<ref name=akita-jinja/><ref name=yuisho/><ref name="akita-shinkou">斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』</ref>. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory<ref name=akita-jinja/>, dedicating a kabura arrow<ref name=yuisho/>.

<gallery> File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya </gallery>
== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge<ref>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>.
== Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain.
By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City.
The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691<ref>{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}</ref>.
In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、{{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014}}、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties ==
{{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu<ref name=yuisho/> * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro<ref name=yuisho/> * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine Ranking system
== Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)<ref name=access>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> *** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]]

== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|lang=ja|title= 東北霊山と修験道 |year= 1979 |publisher= 名著出版 |series= 山岳宗教史研究叢書 7 |isbn= 978-4626001702 |ref= {{SfnRef|東北霊山と修験道}} }} * {{Cite book|lang=ja|title= 秋田大百科事典 |year= 1981 |publisher= [[秋田魁新報社]] |isbn= 4870200074 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Taiheizan-Miyoshi-jinja]][[ja:太平山三吉神社]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja Shaden.jpg |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita |location = {{ill|Akita|WD=Q17139|1=Akita (city)}} |coordinates = {{Coord|39.726167|N|140.144389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}}
'''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)<ref name="akita-jinja">田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』</ref><ref name=yuisho>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice<ref name=yuisho/>.
== History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu<ref name=akita-jinja/><ref name=yuisho/><ref name="akita-shinkou">斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』</ref>. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory<ref name=akita-jinja/>, dedicating a kabura arrow<ref name=yuisho/>.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity<ref name=yuisho/>. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu<ref name=akita-jinja/>. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka<ref name=yuisho/>.
The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide<ref name=akita-jinja/>. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread<ref name=akita-shinkou/><ref name=yuisho/>.
== festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bonten Matsuri|1=Q11538775|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538775}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)<ref name=matsuri>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> * May 8: Spring Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * July 17: Opening Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * September 17: Closing Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/>
== Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) ** Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin ** founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami ** founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami ** founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown
<gallery> File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya </gallery>
== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge<ref>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>.
== Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain.
By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City.
The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691<ref>{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}</ref>.
In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、{{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014|1=2014}}、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties ==

'''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit.

Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice<ref name=yuisho/>.
== History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu<ref name=akita-jinja/><ref name=yuisho/><ref name="akita-shinkou">斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』</ref>. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory<ref name=akita-jinja/>, dedicating a kabura arrow<ref name=yuisho/>.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity<ref name=yuisho/>. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu<ref name=akita-jinja/>. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka<ref name=yuisho/>.
The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide<ref name=akita-jinja/>. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread<ref name=akita-shinkou/><ref name=yuisho/>.
== festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bonten Matsuri|1=Q11538775|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538775}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)<ref name=matsuri>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> * May 8: Spring Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * July 17: Opening Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * September 17: Closing Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/>
== Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) ** Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin ** founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami ** founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami ** founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown
<gallery> File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya </gallery>
== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge<ref>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>.
== Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain.
By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City.
The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691<ref>{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}</ref>.
In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、{{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014|1=2014}}、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties ==

== Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)<ref name=access>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> *** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine |image = File:Taiheizan Miyoshi Jinja Shaden.jpg |location = 3-2 Akenuma, Hiromote, Akita City, Akita Prefecture |LatDeg = 39 | LatMin = 43 | LatSec = 34.2 |LonDeg = 140 | LonMin = 08 | LonSec = 39.8 |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine, Special Shrine |founded = Traditionally Hakuhō 2 (673) |main hall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage Sites = |festival = May 8, October 17 |Rituals = |map = Japan Akita |location = {{ill|Akita|WD=Q17139|1=Akita (city)}} |coordinates = {{Coord|39.726167|N|140.144389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|39.726167|140.144389}}|caption=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|width=256}}
'''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)<ref name="akita-jinja">田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』</ref><ref name=yuisho>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice<ref name=yuisho/>.
== History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu<ref name=akita-jinja/><ref name=yuisho/><ref name="akita-shinkou">斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』</ref>. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory<ref name=akita-jinja/>, dedicating a kabura arrow<ref name=yuisho/>.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity<ref name=yuisho/>. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu<ref name=akita-jinja/>. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka<ref name=yuisho/>.
The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide<ref name=akita-jinja/>. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread<ref name=akita-shinkou/><ref name=yuisho/>.
== festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bonten Matsuri|1=Q11538775|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538775}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)<ref name=matsuri>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> * May 8: Spring Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * July 17: Opening Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * September 17: Closing Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/>
== Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) ** Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin ** founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami ** founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami ** founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown
<gallery> File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya </gallery>
== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge<ref>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>.

'''Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine''' (太平山三吉神社) is a Shinto shrine located in Akita City, Akita Prefecture. The shrine is dedicated to mountain worship. The Satomiya Shrine is located at the foot of Mount Taihei, while the Okumiya Shrine is at the summit.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Miyoshi Reijin (みよしのおおかみ)<ref name="akita-jinja">田村泰造「太平山三吉神社」『秋田大百科事典』</ref><ref name=yuisho>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/index.html 由緒・御deity] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>. Miyoshi Reijin is said to be the deified form of the local lord Fujiwara no Miyoshi (Tsuruju-maru), but is primarily a god associated with mountain worship, symbolizing power, victory, and justice<ref name=yuisho/>.
== History == According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in Hakuhō 2 (673) by En no Ozunu<ref name=akita-jinja/><ref name=yuisho/><ref name="akita-shinkou">斎藤寿胤「太平山信仰」『秋田大百科事典』</ref>. Later, in Enryaku 20 (801), General Sakanoue no Tamuramaro established a shrine building after praying for victory<ref name=akita-jinja/>, dedicating a kabura arrow<ref name=yuisho/>.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered as a sacred place of Shugendō with Yakushi Nyorai as the principal deity<ref name=yuisho/>. In the early modern period, the Akita Domain lords of the Satake clan also donated shrine lands, and the current Satomiya was built by the 8th lord Satake Yoshinobu<ref name=akita-jinja/>. During the Boshin War, it served as a prayer site for the Northern Pacification Governor Kujo Michitaka<ref name=yuisho/>.
The shrine continues to be venerated throughout the Tōhoku region, and Miyoshi shrines can be found nationwide<ref name=akita-jinja/>. Taiheizan Kō and Miyoshi Kō are widespread<ref name=akita-shinkou/><ref name=yuisho/>.
== festivals == * January 17: {{ill|Bonten Matsuri|ja|梵天まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bonten Matsuri|1=Q11538775|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538775}} (Three Gods Bonten festival)<ref name=matsuri>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/festival/index.html 主なお祭] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> * May 8: Spring Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * July 17: Opening Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Satomiya to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei) * August 8: Kōsha Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/> * September 17: Closing Mountain festival<ref name=matsuri/> (the deities are transferred from the Okumiya to the Satomiya) * October 17: Autumn Grand festival<ref name=matsuri/>
== Outer Shrine == * '''Okumiya''' (Mount Taihei Summit, Akita City, Taiheizan Tani aza Taniyama 21, Akita City, Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Mount Taihei Summit {{Coord|39|47|49.5|N|140|18|39.5|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya}}) ** Deities: Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, Miyoshi Reijin ** founded: Traditionally Hakuhō 2 * '''Kisokichi-yama Shrine''' (Mount Nakadake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|46|47.0|N|140|16|14.6|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Kisokichi-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kanayamahiko no Kami, Kanayamahime no Kami ** founded: Kōka 3 (1846) * '''Shinsen-yama Shrine''' (Mount Maedake Summit, Akita City, Taihei Hatta aza Yabitsu {{Coord|39|47|00.2|N|140|15|23.9|E|scale:25000|【map】|name=Shinsen-yama Shrine}}) ** Deities: Kotoshironushi no Kami, Ōwatatsumi no Kami, Sarutahiko no Kami ** founded: Before the Kōka era * '''Mitarai Shrine''' (1 Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown * '''Mitaki Shrine''' (Nibetsu aza Mutsuzawa, Akita City) ** Deities: Kagutsuchi no Kami, Haraedo no Kami ** founded: Unknown
<gallery> File:太平山神社.jpg|Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine Okumiya </gallery>
== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season. Reservations are required for lodging and meals, while rest is free of charge<ref>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/okumiya 奥宮と山小屋] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref>.
== Taiheizan Belief == Mount Taihei is revered as a deity itself. The shrine's deities Miyoshi Reijin is worshiped as a god of strength, victory, business, agriculture, and exams. Miyoshi Reijin is believed to have become a god through ascetic practices on the mountain. Sugae Masumi noted the similarity between Mount Taihei and Yoshino, suggesting that the name Miyoshi might originate from Yoshino<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The specific period when Mount Taihei was named is unknown. The mountain was also known as Kinpōzan, Yakushi-mine, Sanbon-ga-dake, and Orochine. The popularization of Taiheizan worship dates back to the mid-Edo period, with plaques commemorating pilgrimages to the mountain erected during the Bunka and Bunsei periods (1803-1830). Unlike other mountains, Taihei did not necessitate guides, and as a result, no Shugendō community was established at the base. Instead, Shugendō was limited to managing the shrine. Taiheizan and Miyoshi shrines gained widespread recognition nationally, partly due to the promotion of Taiheizan sake and the prominence of the Bonten festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=271-272}}</ref>.
The Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei enshrines Ōkuninushi, Sukunabikona, and Miyoshi Reijin. It is said to have been founded by En no Ozunu in Hakuhō 2 and rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro in Enryaku 2. It was designated as a Gōsha (village shrine) in 1873, elevated to a Prefectural Shrine in 1879, and renamed Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine in 1906. A Satomiya was authorized in 1913. The popularity of Taiheizan worship surged in the late Edo period, with numerous small shrines and stone chapels dedicated to Miyoshi or Taiheizan built across the Satake Domain.
By the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the number of these shrines had dwindled due to shrine mergers. Local mini-Taiheizan shrines emerged as substitutes, including hills named Taiheizan in regions such as Kawabe District, Senboku District, and Oomagari City.
The oldest records of Miyoshi worship include writings by Tadanomakoto and Sugae Masumi. Tadanomakoto's ''Mukashibanashi'' depicts Miyoshi as a "stranger" who grants wishes in exchange for sake, while Sugae Masumi's ''Tsukinoorochine'' tells of a wrestler gaining strength through offerings to Miyoshi. These writings highlight Miyoshi's association with strength. The earliest evidence of Miyoshi being considered a hermit deity is from the Edo period, with a plaque in Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine inscribed with "Hermit Miyoshi Gongen" dating back to 1691<ref>{{Cite web|url= https://www.akita-u.ac.jp/honbu/global/en/abroad/inbound/pdf/report_2021_01.pdf |title= 「講」から「協力会」へ―太平山三吉神社の信仰組織 |author= 呉柱賢 |publisher= 秋田大学 |accessdate= 2024-03-09 }}</ref>.
In the mid-early modern period, Taiheizan worship became established among the common people of Akita Domain (Kubota). During the Bunka and Bunsei periods, village associations for Taiheizan worship were formed. These associations persist today, with 85 groups regularly visiting the shrine as of 1999-2000, the majority of which are from Akita Prefecture<ref>『日本の霊山読み解き事典』、西海賢二・時枝務・久野俊彦、{{ill|2014|en|2014|WD=Q1999|lt=2014|1=2014}}、p.106－109、柏書房</ref>.
In 1979, the Taihei Kō in Kajime, Soto Koto Akita, was primarily a recreational group focused on summer mountain climbing. Meetings included chanting and offering food at a local shrine. The Hachirōgata region saw a proliferation of Taihei Kō, with some groups reciting traditional prayers and participating in the Bon festival<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=281-282}}</ref>.
During the Akita War, monks prayed for the enemy's retreat at Taiheizan. The Kodama Brewery in Iidagawa produced a sake called "Kongō" but later rebranded it as "Taiheizan," leading to increased popularity and spreading the shrine's fame<ref>{{Sfn|東北霊山と修験道|pp=282}}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties ==
{{Stub Section}} == Timeline == * Hakuhō 2 (673): Traditionally founded by En no Ozunu<ref name=yuisho/> * Enryaku 20 (801): Traditionally rebuilt by Sakanoue no Tamuramaro<ref name=yuisho/> * Keichō 7 (1602): Satake Yoshinobu, Lord of Akita, establishes the shrine. * March, 1868 (Meiji 1): Separation of Buddhism and Shinto * May 14, 1871 (Meiji 4): Establishment of the shrine Ranking system
== Access == Approximately 4 kilometers from [[Akita Chūō Interchange|Akita Chūō IC]], along Akita Prefectural Route 28 (Akita Iwami Funaoka Line). === Bus === * Miyoshi Shrine Entrance bus stop (from Akita Station, University Hospital Line / Taihei Hatta Line)<ref name=access>[http://www.miyoshi.or.jp/information/access.html アクセス] - Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine</ref> *** [[Akita Chūō Kōtsū]]

== Lodging == Adjacent to the Okumiya at the summit of Mount Taihei is a lodging facility (mountain hut) where shrine officials are stationed during the summer season.
